items.q: What is the difference between ozone depletion and climate change?
items.correct: Two distinct issues - ozone depletion is about UV protection (caused mainly by CFCs); climate change is about heat trapping (caused mainly by CO2).
items.random_wrong: Ozone depletion causes climate change directly.
items.why: Students conflate these as 'the atmosphere problem'. They involve different gases, different atmospheric layers, different mechanisms, and different (though sometimes connected) environmental consequences.

items.q: What process moves carbon from the atmosphere into plants?
items.why: Students confuse the directions. Photosynthesis takes CO2 OUT of the atmosphere (plants build sugars); respiration RELEASES CO2. Both run simultaneously in plants, but only photosynthesis fixes new carbon.

items.q: Why is burning fossil fuels considered to disrupt the carbon cycle?
items.correct: It releases carbon stored for millions of years much faster than the cycle can absorb.
items.misconception: It creates new carbon that did not exist before.
items.why: Students think combustion creates carbon. It RELEASES previously stored carbon - the issue is rate. Geological storage took millions of years; we're releasing it in centuries, overwhelming natural sinks.

items.q: Stratospheric ozone is concentrated at altitudes of:
items.why: Correct answer: ~15–35 km. “~0–10 km” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Ozone column thickness. “UV intensity” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If a stratospheric ozone column measures 250 DU, the equivalent layer thickness at STP (mm) is?
items.why: Exact answer: 2.5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
